title: Organising
summary: Organising is the process of defining and grouping activities, assigning duties, delegating authority, and establishing relationships to achieve organisational goals.
Organising process: Identify activities → Group activities → Assign duties → Delegate authority → Establish relationships.
Two types of organisational structure: Functional (grouped by functions — purchase, production, marketing) and Divisional (grouped by products — garments, shoes).
Formal organisation: Deliberately created, well-defined authority, rigid. Informal organisation: Arises spontaneously from social interactions, flexible, satisfies social needs.
Delegation: Entrusting authority and responsibility to subordinates. Elements: Authority, Responsibility, Accountability.
Decentralisation: Systematic delegation to lower levels across the organisation. More autonomy to middle/lower management.
This chapter carries 8 marks — one of the highest weightage chapters.

teacher_tip.title: AKSpected Shortcut
teacher_tip.text: Delegation = one person to another (manager to subordinate). Decentralisation = top to entire organisation (systematic, organisation-wide). If the question says 'subordinate cannot further delegate' → Accountability (it always flows upward). If 'product-wise divisions' → Divisional structure. If 'function-wise departments' → Functional structure.

sections.title: Meaning of Organising

definition.text: The process of identifying and grouping the work to be performed, defining and delegating authority and responsibility, and establishing relationships for enabling people to work most effectively together.

sections.content: Organising follows planning. Once plans are made, organising decides who will do what, with what authority, and how activities will be coordinated.

sections.title: Organising Process
points: 1. Identify and Divide Work: Break total work into specific jobs and activities.
2. Group Activities (Departmentalisation): Group similar activities together into departments.
3. Assign Duties: Allocate work to individuals based on their skills and competence.
4. Delegate Authority: Give each person the right to make decisions within their area.
5. Establish Reporting Relationships: Define who reports to whom — creates the organisational hierarchy.

sections.title: Functional Structure
sections.content: Activities are grouped by functions — Production, Marketing, Finance, HR, etc. Each department is headed by a specialist.
points: Suitable for: Large organisations with a single product line.
Advantages: Specialisation, better coordination within departments, managerial efficiency.
Disadvantages: Departmental heads may prioritise their department over organisational goals, poor coordination between departments.

sections.title: Divisional Structure
sections.content: Activities are grouped by products, regions, or customer types. Each division operates as a semi-autonomous unit with its own functional departments.
points: Suitable for: Multi-product companies (e.g., garments + shoes divisions).
Advantages: Product specialisation, quick decision-making, divisional accountability.
Disadvantages: Duplication of resources across divisions, higher cost, conflicts between divisions.

sections.title: Formal vs Informal Organisation
points: Formal: Deliberately created by management. Well-defined authority, rules, and procedures. Rigid structure. Focus: achieving organisational goals.
Informal: Arises spontaneously from social interactions. No fixed rules. Flexible. Satisfies social and personal needs. Example: Lunch group of employees from different departments.
Key point: Both co-exist in every organisation. Informal organisation fills communication gaps of formal organisation (grapevine). A manager should use both effectively.

sections.title: Delegation
sections.content: Delegation means entrusting part of the work and authority to a subordinate while retaining overall responsibility.
points: Elements of Delegation:
1. Authority: The right to give orders and make decisions. Flows downward.
2. Responsibility: The obligation to perform the assigned task. Also flows downward.
3. Accountability: The answerability for the outcome. Always flows upward. Cannot be delegated — the delegator remains ultimately accountable.
Importance: Reduces manager's workload, develops subordinates, motivates employees, enables quick decisions, better coordination.

sections.title: Decentralisation
sections.content: Decentralisation is the systematic delegation of authority to all levels of management throughout the organisation. It extends delegation to the organisational level.
points: Decentralisation ≠ Delegation: Delegation is between two individuals; decentralisation is organisation-wide policy.
Importance: Develops initiative in subordinates, quick and better decisions, relief to top management, facilitates growth, democratic management.
Example: Panchayati Raj system — government decentralises power to local bodies.

tables.title: Functional vs Divisional Structure
tables.tip: Very common 4-mark question. Know all 4 differences.
rows: Difficult to fix for specific product

tables.title: Delegation vs Decentralisation
tables.tip: Another guaranteed 4-mark question. Learn all differences.
rows: Compulsory — manager must delegate to function
Departmental heads accountable for their division

questions.text: Which element of delegation cannot be further delegated?
questions.answer: Accountability. It always flows upward — the delegator remains ultimately answerable.

questions.text: State any three advantages of delegation.
questions.answer:
(i) Reduces workload of the manager, allowing focus on important tasks.
(ii) Develops subordinates by giving them exposure to decision-making.
(iii) Enables quick decisions as subordinates can act without waiting for approval.

questions.text: Distinguish between Functional and Divisional structure.
questions.answer:
(1) Functional groups activities by function; Divisional groups by product.
(2) Functional has functional specialisation; Divisional has product specialisation.
(3) Functional: hard to fix product accountability; Divisional: each division is accountable.
(4) Functional suits single-product firms; Divisional suits multi-product companies.

questions.text:
A company manufacturing motorcycles and cars has two separate divisions, each with its own production, marketing, and finance departments. Each division head plans and executes independently.

Identify the organisational structure and explain two advantages.
questions.answer:
Divisional Structure. The company groups activities by product (motorcycles and cars), each operating as a semi-autonomous unit.

Advantages:
(1) Product specialisation — each division focuses entirely on its product, improving quality and innovation.
(2) Easy accountability — performance of each division can be measured separately, making it clear which product line is profitable.

pyqs.answer:
(1) Delegation is between two individuals; Decentralisation is organisation-wide.
(2) Delegation has less freedom; Decentralisation gives greater autonomy.
(3) Delegation is compulsory; Decentralisation is optional.
(4) In delegation, the delegator is accountable; in decentralisation, departmental heads are accountable.

pyqs.text: State any three features of formal organisation.
pyqs.answer:
(i) Deliberately created by management with well-defined rules.
(ii) Clear hierarchy of authority and responsibility.
(iii) Focuses on achieving organisational goals through official channels.

related_chapters.title: Staffing
related_chapters.reason: Staffing fills the roles created by organising

mnemonics.title: Elements of Delegation — A-R-A
mnemonics.text: Authority (flows down) → Responsibility (flows down) → Accountability (flows UP — cannot be delegated)
